quotes.verbatim_quote:
These structures are called “Chord Forms" because they move from one form to an-
other by sustaining certain tones and moving others freely up or down the finger board, as
seen in examples 1 through 4 below.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the foundational definition of the method's core concept — Chord Forms — explaining the mechanism of voice leading that underpins all four examples.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Your fourth finger plays a major part in forming these structures, therefore, the examples
below are to be practiced chromatically from the first to the tenth fret on your guitar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This gives both a fingering rule (fourth finger is load-bearing) and a practice prescription (chromatic, frets 1-10) that governs how all four exercises should be executed.

quotes.verbatim_quote: * Notice that the bottom tone of each chord moves down a whole tone.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the key voice-leading rule that defines the characteristic harmonic motion across all four chord-form exercises in the lesson.
